title: Comparing air-dry, paper, and polymer clay
subtitle: A reference walk through the three non-fired clay bodies, their working windows, and what each is for
excerpt: A reference tutorial for choosing between the three clay bodies that work without a kiln: air-dry clay, paper clay (air-dry), and polymer clay. Each behaves differently in the hand, sets in a different way, and finishes for a different use. This tutorial walks through the working window, the strength after setting, and the finishes each accepts.
sourceNotes: Comparison drawn from the published handling characteristics of the three body families. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry, for the historical context of cellulose-bound clay. Polymer-clay handling notes derived from first principles and the manufacturer-published curing schedule (110-130°C for 15-30 minutes), standard across the major polymer clay families.

glossaryTerms.definition: The amount of time clay stays soft enough to shape after it is taken out of the packet or worked. Different clay bodies have very different windows.
The heat process that sets polymer clay in a domestic oven at 110-130°C, fusing the PVC particles into a hard plastic.
Fully air-dried but unfired. For air-dry and paper clay, this is the finished structural state.

aliases: which clay to use no kiln

content.text: Three clay bodies in the publication set without a kiln: air-dry clay, paper clay (air-dry), and polymer clay. Each looks and feels broadly similar in the hand, but they behave differently under work, set in different ways, and finish for different uses. This reference walks through how each one works and when to reach for it.
Air-dry clay

Air-dry clay is a water-based clay body with a cellulose binder. It feels and behaves like traditional kiln-fired clay while it is wet: it slips, pinches, coils, and joins with slurry. The body sets by losing water at room temperature, taking 1 to 3 days to reach
bone-dry
depending on wall thickness.
The
working window

is open as long as the clay stays under plastic or in its packaging. Once the surface starts drying, the clay loses workability fast: 20-40 minutes of open air and the outer skin firms enough to crack under further shaping. A spritz of water and a few minutes under plastic returns the surface to a workable softness, up to a point.

Finished air-dry pieces are porous and not waterproof. They take paint, ink, marker, and pencil. Sealing with PVA or acrylic varnish gives a wipeable surface but does not make the piece truly water-tight. Best for decorative work: pinch pots, slab tiles, coil-built vessels, sculpture, ornaments. Not for drinking vessels, planters that hold soil, or anything that needs to live outdoors.
Paper clay (air-dry)

Paper clay is an air-dry clay body with paper fibres mixed through it. The fibres act as a tiny scaffolding, bridging joins and resisting the cracks that plain air-dry clay can develop in larger or more complex pieces. Wet, it handles slightly differently from plain air-dry: a touch coarser to the touch from the visible fibre, slightly stronger at the green stage, more forgiving of joining wet clay onto dry.
The working window is similar to plain air-dry clay. Drying time runs longer because the fibres hold water inside the body, typically 2 to 3 days to bone-dry depending on wall thickness.

Paper clay is the body of choice for any piece where joins must hold under stress: a handle on a tankard, the seams of a slab box, a sculptural form with thin extending elements. The added strength at the green stage means fewer cracked pieces during drying. The fibre is invisible once painted or sealed. The same limitations apply as plain air-dry: porous, not waterproof, decorative.
Polymer clay

Polymer clay is not a true ceramic. It is a PVC-based modelling material that sets in a domestic oven at 110-130°C in 15-30 minutes. The cured piece is a hard plastic, not a fired ceramic. The material handles unlike water-based clays: it stays workable indefinitely as long as it is at room temperature, softening with the warmth of the hands and stiffening when cool. It does not dry in air.

The working window is effectively unlimited until the piece goes into the oven. A polymer-clay project can be set aside for days and returned to without any loss of workability. Conditioning (warming and folding the clay before use) softens a fresh block to working consistency in two or three minutes.
Once cured, polymer clay is rigid, water-resistant, and hard-wearing at small scale. It is not food-safe, so cured pieces should not contact food directly.
Curing

is done on a dedicated tray kept for clay work, not shared with food cookware. Best for small detailed work: beads, jewellery, miniatures, sculptural small forms, buttons, keychains. Less suitable for larger flat work because cured polymer can become brittle at thin large-area sections.
Choosing between them
For a beginner project at any scale, plain air-dry clay is the most forgiving entry: tolerant of small mistakes, no oven step, lowest cost per gram. Pinch pots, coil work, flat tiles.

For larger or more structural air-dry work, paper clay gives back stronger joins and fewer cracks. The extra cost is worth it on any piece with a handle, a stretched element, or thin slab joins. Coil tankards, slab boxes, hanging mobiles.

For small detailed pieces where the finish needs to be hard-wearing (jewellery, keychains, beads, miniature decorative objects), polymer clay is the right choice. The oven curing step trades a half hour at the cooker for a rigid finished piece.
What all three share

None is food-safe in the strict ceramic sense. Air-dry and paper clay pieces stay porous after sealing; polymer clay is plastic and not certified for food contact. None is suitable for use as a drinking vessel, a serving dish, or a planter that holds soil and water. All three give a satisfying material to work with at home without needing a kiln, and each opens a different shape of finished object.
Common mistakes
Treating polymer clay like air-dry. Polymer clay does not dry; leaving a polymer piece out overnight does nothing. It only sets in the oven.

Joining wet air-dry clay to bone-dry air-dry clay. The wet clay shrinks as it dries; the dry clay does not. The join fails. Paper clay tolerates this better than plain air-dry but neither tolerates it well.
Expecting air-dry clay to hold water. Even sealed, the body remains porous over time. A vase finished in air-dry clay holds dried botanicals well; fresh-flower water seeps through after a day.
